title: Authentication vs Authorization
description:
## Two Words People Constantly Mix Up

In IAM, **authentication** and **authorization** are distinct steps, and **Single Sign-On (SSO)** is something different again.

Which statement describes these terms **correctly**?

options.text: Authentication decides *what* you are allowed to do; authorization proves *who* you are.
Authentication proves *who you are*; authorization decides *what you are allowed to do*; SSO lets you sign in once and reach many apps.
Authentication and authorization are the same thing, and SSO simply means using a strong password.
Authorization proves *who you are*; SSO means each app stores its own copy of your password.

explanation: **Authentication (authN)** answers *who are you?* — verifying identity, e.g. by checking a password or an MFA device. **Authorization (authZ)** answers *what may you do?* — checking roles, scopes, or permissions once identity is established. **SSO** means a single authentication establishes a session that multiple applications trust, so the user signs in once and accesses many apps. Option **a** swaps the two definitions. Option **c** wrongly conflates them (and SSO is not just a strong password). Option **d** assigns identity proof to authorization and describes the opposite of SSO (SSO avoids each app storing credentials).

hints.title: Order matters
hints.body: Authentication always happens first — you must establish identity before a system can decide what that identity is permitted to do.
